# MicroCommerce

## What This Is

A showcase e-commerce platform demonstrating modern .NET microservices architecture with DDD tactical patterns. Users can browse products, manage profiles, write verified reviews, save wishlists, add items to cart, and complete purchases through a polished Next.js storefront backed by a modular monolith with event-driven architecture, CQRS, and a comprehensive DDD building block foundation (strongly-typed IDs, SmartEnums, Result pattern, Specifications, interceptor-driven audit/concurrency/soft-delete).

## Core Value

**A user can complete a purchase end-to-end** — browse products, add to cart, checkout as a guest, and see their order confirmation. Everything else supports this flow.

## Requirements

### Validated

- ✓ Product catalog with categories, search, and infinite scroll — v1.0
- ✓ Product detail pages with stock display and add-to-cart — v1.0
- ✓ Shopping cart (persistent, guest-friendly, optimistic UI) — v1.0
- ✓ Checkout flow with mock payment and saga orchestration — v1.0
- ✓ Order confirmation and order history with real-time status — v1.0
- ✓ Inventory tracking with stock reservations and TTL — v1.0
- ✓ Admin UI for product management, inventory, and orders — v1.0
- ✓ Admin dashboard with stat cards, charts, and Kanban board — v1.0
- ✓ Seed data with 50 products across 8 categories — v1.0
- ✓ API Gateway (YARP) with JWT auth and rate limiting — v1.0
- ✓ Event-driven communication (MassTransit + Azure Service Bus) — v1.0
- ✓ CQRS pattern with MediatR and validation pipeline — v1.0
- ✓ Database-per-feature isolation with separate DbContexts — v1.0
- ✓ 180 automated tests (unit, integration, E2E) — v1.0
- ✓ Keycloak authentication (backend JWT + NextAuth.js) — existing
- ✓ .NET Aspire orchestration — existing
- ✓ DDD building blocks (aggregates, domain events, value objects) — existing
- ✓ OpenTelemetry observability — existing
- ✓ User profiles with display name, avatar, address book — v1.1
- ✓ Guest-to-authenticated cart and order merge on login — v1.1
- ✓ Product reviews with verified purchase enforcement (star ratings + text) — v1.1
- ✓ Aggregate star ratings and review counts on product pages — v1.1
- ✓ Wishlists with add/remove, move-to-cart, heart icon indicators — v1.1
- ✓ E2E test coverage for user features (guest, authenticated, navigation) — v1.1
- ✓ DDD audit (71 findings) with value object migration to record structs — v1.1
- ✓ CQRS compliance fixes and aggregate boundary enforcement — v1.1
- ✓ Entity hierarchy (Entity<TId>, AuditableAggregateRoot<TId>) with typed ID child entities — v2.0
- ✓ Vogen source-generated strongly-typed IDs (14 types) with auto EF Core + JSON converters — v2.0
- ✓ EF Core conventions for StronglyTypedId auto-conversion and ConcurrencyToken auto-config — v2.0
- ✓ SmartEnum state machines for OrderStatus (8 states) and ProductStatus (3 states) — v2.0
- ✓ Result pattern (FluentResults) with dual MediatR validation pipeline and HTTP mapping — v2.0
- ✓ Specification pattern (Ardalis.Specification) with 8 composable query specs — v2.0
- ✓ AuditInterceptor auto-setting CreatedAt/UpdatedAt on IAuditable entities — v2.0
- ✓ ConcurrencyInterceptor with explicit Version column replacing xmin — v2.0
- ✓ SoftDeleteInterceptor infrastructure wired (ready for future entity adoption) — v2.0
- ✓ Integration testing with Testcontainers + WebApplicationFactory (182 tests) — v2.0
- ✓ Full building block adoption across all 7 feature modules — v2.0
- ✓ OpenAPI schema transformers for Vogen IDs and SmartEnums — v2.0
- ✓ Obsolete ValueObject base class and StronglyTypedIdConvention removed — v2.0
- ✓ Dockerfiles for ApiService (chiseled .NET), Gateway (chiseled .NET), Web (standalone Node.js) — v3.0
- ✓ Kustomize manifests (base + dev overlay) for all services and infrastructure — v3.0
- ✓ Full stack deployment in K8s: apps + PostgreSQL + Keycloak + RabbitMQ — v3.0
- ✓ RabbitMQ transport support for MassTransit (MASSTRANSIT_TRANSPORT env var switching) — v3.0
- ✓ GitHub Actions CI pipeline building and pushing images to ghcr.io — v3.0
- ✓ ArgoCD with app-of-apps pattern for GitOps deployment (self-heal + prune) — v3.0
- ✓ Sealed Secrets for K8s secret management — v3.0
- ✓ CI/CD GitOps loop: CI commits SHA tags → ArgoCD auto-syncs — v3.0
- ✓ OTEL Collector + Aspire Dashboard deployed in K8s for monitoring — v3.0
- ✓ Local kind cluster as dev environment with bootstrap.sh — v3.0
- ✓ Complete UI refresh: 11 pages restyled with shadcn/ui, oklch tokens, DM Sans — v3.0
- ✓ Runtime API URL resolution for client-side K8s deployment — v3.0

### Active

(No active milestone — use `/gsd:new-milestone` to start next)

### Out of Scope

- Real payment processing (Stripe, etc.) — mock payments sufficient for demo
- Event sourcing — adds complexity without proportional demo value
- Separate admin application — integrated admin routes simpler
- Mobile app — web-first, responsive design covers mobile
- Multi-tenancy — single store demonstration
- Internationalization — English only for now
- Anonymous reviews — trust collapse risk; verified purchase enforcement is core
- Public user profiles — privacy concern; profiles are private to account owner
- AI review summaries — requires LLM integration, cost analysis
- Review image moderation — requires moderation service
- Generic Repository wrapper — EF Core DbContext already is unit-of-work + repository
- Event sourcing primitives — adds complexity without proportional demo value
- CreatedBy/ModifiedBy user tracking — requires IHttpContextAccessor integration, defer to future

## Context

**Shipped v3.0 Kubernetes & GitOps** with 25.6K LOC C# backend, 15.7K LOC TypeScript frontend, 1.3K LOC K8s manifests. Full stack deployed to kind cluster via ArgoCD GitOps.

**Tech stack:**
- Backend: .NET 10, ASP.NET Core Minimal APIs, .NET Aspire 13.1.0
- Frontend: Next.js 16, React 19, TypeScript 5, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS v4
- Auth: Keycloak (backend JWT + NextAuth.js v5 frontend)
- Database: PostgreSQL (per-feature isolation, 8 DbContexts)
- Messaging: MassTransit 9.0 (Azure Service Bus for Aspire dev, RabbitMQ for K8s)
- Gateway: YARP reverse proxy with JWT, rate limiting, CORS
- Deployment: Kubernetes (kind), Kustomize, ArgoCD, Sealed Secrets, GitHub Actions CI
- Observability: OpenTelemetry → OTEL Collector → Aspire Dashboard
- Patterns: CQRS (MediatR), DDD, Event-Driven, Vertical Slice Architecture
- DDD Building Blocks: Vogen StronglyTypedId, SmartEnum, FluentResults, Ardalis.Specification, EF Core Interceptors
- Testing: xUnit, FluentAssertions, Testcontainers, WebApplicationFactory, Playwright

**Architecture:** Modular monolith with clear bounded contexts (Catalog, Cart, Ordering, Inventory, Profiles, Reviews, Wishlists, Messaging). Full DDD building block foundation adopted across all modules. Deployed to Kubernetes via GitOps with full CI/CD automation.

**Known tech debt:**
- Hardcoded configuration (stock thresholds, shipping rates)
- Placeholder images (placehold.co URLs; Azurite images won't resolve in K8s — needs STOR-01)
- Cross-context DbContext injection in Reviews and Wishlists modules (bounded context isolation violation)
- ProductStatus missing `[JsonConverter]` attribute (server-side works, latent client-side gap)
- DomainEventInterceptor uses scoped DI (not wired via AddInterceptors — needs IPublishEndpoint)
- ISoftDeletable has zero entity adopters (infrastructure ready, no entities implement it yet)
- AUTH_URL=localhost:38800 works for local kind dev only (needs adjustment for remote clusters)
- Hero image placeholder (gray bg-muted div)

## Constraints

- **Tech Stack**: .NET 10 + Aspire for backend, Next.js 16 for frontend — committed
- **Identity**: Keycloak — integrated, don't duplicate auth
- **Message Broker**: MassTransit with Azure SB (Aspire) / RabbitMQ (K8s)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL — in deployment manifests
- **Deployment**: Kubernetes with ArgoCD GitOps

## Key Decisions

| Decision | Rationale | Outcome |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Gradual service extraction | Real-world pattern, demonstrates monolith-to-microservices journey | ✓ Good — clean module boundaries established |
| Database-per-feature | True microservices isolation, showcases data ownership | ✓ Good — 8 separate DbContexts working well |
| Guest checkout | Lower friction, simpler v1 scope | ✓ Good — cookie-based buyer identity works seamlessly |
| Mock payments | Avoid payment processor complexity while demonstrating flow | ✓ Good — toggleable success/failure |
| Cart in Postgres (not Redis) | Durability over speed, consistent with database-per-service | ✓ Good — reliable with TTL cleanup |
| Admin in main app | Simpler deployment, shared auth context | ✓ Good — route-based separation clean |
| shadcn/ui | Modern, Tailwind-based, great DX | ✓ Good — consistent design across storefront and admin |
| MassTransit + transactional outbox | Reliable event delivery with at-least-once guarantees | ✓ Good — checkout saga compensation works |
| Checkout saga (MassTransit state machine) | Orchestrate multi-step checkout with compensation | ✓ Good — handles stock reservation, payment, cart clearing |
| YARP API Gateway | Unified entry point, security centralization | ✓ Good — JWT + rate limiting at edge |
| Testcontainers for integration tests | Real database testing without Docker Compose | ✓ Good — catches real SQL/EF issues |
| ImageSharp for avatar processing | Crop-to-square and resize to 400x400 | ✓ Good — no external service needed |
| Auto-create profile on first GET | Profile always exists for authenticated users | ✓ Good — eliminates null profile edge cases |
| Silent cart merge on login | Guest cart transfers to authenticated user without intervention | ✓ Good — seamless UX |
| Verified purchase reviews | Reviews require order with Paid/Confirmed/Shipped/Delivered status | ✓ Good — trust through enforcement |
| Denormalized review stats | AverageRating and ReviewCount on Product entity | ✓ Good — fast queries, synchronous recalculation |
| Optimistic wishlist UI | Heart icon toggles immediately with cache rollback on error | ✓ Good — instant feedback |
| Readonly record structs for value objects | Compiler-generated equality, 20x faster than reflection-based ValueObject | ✓ Good — modern C# semantics |
| ComplexProperty for struct value objects | Avoids EF Core shadow primary key generation from OwnsOne | ✓ Good — cleaner mapping |
| Vernon's aggregate rules as audit standard | 4 rules for aggregate design validation | ✓ Good — caught 21 critical violations |
| Reference by identity only (Vernon Rule 3) | Product references Category by CategoryId, not navigation property | ✓ Good — proper aggregate isolation |
| Vogen for StronglyTypedId | Source-generated record structs vs hand-rolled base class | ✓ Good — zero runtime overhead, auto converters |
| SmartEnum for status types | Behavior-rich enumerations vs plain enum + switch | ✓ Good — state transitions encapsulated, no magic strings |
| FluentResults for explicit errors | Result<T> vs exceptions for domain errors | ✓ Good — 4 handlers adopted, HTTP mapping clean |
| Ardalis.Specification for queries | Composable specs vs inline LINQ | ✓ Good — 8 specs, chained composition works |
| Stateless interceptors as singletons | new() instances vs DI-resolved scoped | ✓ Good — no constructor deps, simpler wiring |
| Interceptor order: SoftDelete → Concurrency → Audit | SoftDelete before Audit so UpdatedAt set on soft-deleted entities | ✓ Good — correct behavioral ordering |
| Testcontainers over in-memory DB | Real PostgreSQL vs EF InMemory provider | ✓ Good — catches real SQL/EF issues, 38 integration tests |
| Order skips AuditableAggregateRoot | Domain-specific CreatedAt/PaidAt semantics | ✓ Good — intentional, documented design choice |

| .NET chiseled containers | Smallest secure images for ApiService/Gateway | ✓ Good — non-root by default, noble-chiseled-extra for ICU |
| Kustomize over Helm | Simpler for single-project, built into kubectl | ✓ Good — base/overlay structure clean |
| ArgoCD app-of-apps | Single root Application manages all services | ✓ Good — self-heal + prune on all 8 child apps |
| Sealed Secrets | Encrypt credentials for Git storage | ✓ Good — bootstrap generates sealed-secret.yaml at runtime |
| MassTransit transport switching | MASSTRANSIT_TRANSPORT env var for Azure SB vs RabbitMQ | ✓ Good — same consumers/saga work on both |
| NodePort for kind access | Avoid Ingress NGINX (EOL March 2026) | ✓ Good — simple port mapping chain |
| Runtime API URL resolution | getApiBase() singleton fetching /api/config at runtime | ✓ Good — single Docker image for all environments |
| OTEL Collector + Aspire Dashboard | Standalone observability without .NET Aspire AppHost | ✓ Good — distributed tracing visible in K8s |

---
*Last updated: 2026-03-03 after v3.0 milestone completed*

# Comprehensive Dockerfile and CI/CD Workflow Review

## Files Reviewed

- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/MicroCommerce.Web/Dockerfile`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/MicroCommerce.Web/.dockerignore`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/.dockerignore`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/Directory.Build.props` (container base image config)
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/.github/workflows/dotnet-test.yml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/.github/workflows/container-images.yml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/.github/workflows/release.yml`

---

## DOCKERFILE: `src/MicroCommerce.Web/Dockerfile`

### GOOD - Multi-stage build with proper separation

The Dockerfile uses a clean 3-stage build pattern (deps, builder, runner). Dependencies are copied before source code, which maximizes layer caching. The `npm ci --ignore-scripts` in the deps stage ensures reproducible installs and avoids malicious post-install scripts.

### GOOD - Non-root user in production stage

Lines 33-41 correctly create a dedicated `nextjs` user (UID 1001) and switch to it before running the application. Files are copied with `--chown=nextjs:nodejs`. This is solid.

### GOOD - Next.js standalone output mode

The `next.config.ts` has `output: 'standalone'` configured, and the Dockerfile correctly copies `standalone` and `static` directories. This produces a minimal production image.

### GOOD - Telemetry disabled

`NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` is set in both the build and runner stages, preventing data leakage.

### WARNING - Hardcoded placeholder secret in build layer (lines 18-19)

```dockerfile
ENV AUTH_SECRET=placeholder-for-docker-build
ENV AUTH_TRUST_HOST=true
```

While the comment explains this is a placeholder overridden at runtime, `ENV` directives are baked into the image layer metadata. Anyone who pulls the image can inspect it with `docker inspect` and see these values. Although the value is "placeholder", this sets a bad precedent and could confuse operators into thinking this is the actual secret. Use `ARG` instead of `ENV` for build-time-only values, since `ARG` values are not persisted in the final image layers:

```dockerfile
ARG AUTH_SECRET=placeholder-for-docker-build
ARG AUTH_TRUST_HOST=true
```

### WARNING - No HEALTHCHECK instruction

The Dockerfile has no `HEALTHCHECK` directive. While Kubernetes liveness/readiness probes typically handle this, a Dockerfile-level health check is valuable for standalone Docker usage and `docker compose` environments. Consider adding:

```dockerfile
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=5s \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1
```

### INFO - Consider pinning the Node.js image digest

`node:22-alpine` is a floating tag. For fully reproducible builds, consider pinning to a digest:

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine@sha256:<digest> AS base
```

This prevents silent base image changes between builds. Dependabot can update the digest automatically.

### INFO - No .npm cache mount for faster CI rebuilds

The `npm ci` step could benefit from a BuildKit cache mount to speed up rebuilds:

```dockerfile
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm npm ci --ignore-scripts
```

---

## .NET Container Publishing (via `dotnet publish /t:PublishContainer`)

### GOOD - Chiseled base image

`Directory.Build.props` line 13 specifies `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:10.0-noble-chiseled-extra` as the base image. Chiseled images are rootless, distroless, and have a minimal attack surface. This is an excellent security choice.

### GOOD - Container configuration centralized in Directory.Build.props

The `ContainerRegistry` and `ContainerBaseImage` properties are defined once in `Directory.Build.props`, and per-service `ContainerRepository` values are in individual `.csproj` files. This is clean and maintainable.

### INFO - No ReadyToRun or trimming configured

The `.csproj` files and `Directory.Build.props` do not enable `PublishReadyToRun`, `PublishTrimmed`, or Native AOT. For a microservices project, enabling ReadyToRun (`<PublishReadyToRun>true</PublishReadyToRun>`) would improve cold-start performance at the cost of slightly larger images. Full trimming or AOT may not be feasible given the heavy use of reflection-based libraries (MediatR, EF Core, MassTransit), so this is informational only.

---

## WORKFLOW: `.github/workflows/dotnet-test.yml`

### CRITICAL - .NET SDK version mismatch

Line 30 specifies `dotnet-version: 9.0.x`, but the project targets `net10.0` (confirmed in `Directory.Build.props` line 4 and all `.csproj` files). This means the test workflow will fail on every run because .NET 9 cannot build .NET 10 projects. This must be updated to `10.0.x`.

### CRITICAL - Aspire workload install is fragile and slow

Lines 33-35:
```yaml
- name: Setup Aspire
  run: |
    dotnet workload update
    dotnet workload install aspire
```

`dotnet workload update` updates ALL workloads, which is slow and non-deterministic. The Aspire workload version is not pinned, so different runs may get different versions. Consider pinning:

```yaml
- name: Setup Aspire
  run: dotnet workload install aspire --version 9.3.0
```

Or better, use the `aspire` workload manifest pinning from a `global.json` file.

### WARNING - No permissions block

The workflow has no `permissions:` declaration. By default, `GITHUB_TOKEN` gets broad read/write permissions. Add a least-privilege permissions block:

```yaml
permissions:
  contents: read
```

### WARNING - No NuGet package caching

The workflow restores NuGet packages from scratch on every run. Add caching:

```yaml
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.nuget/packages
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-nuget-${{ hashFiles('**/*.csproj', '**/Directory.Build.props') }}
    restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-nuget-
```

Alternatively, `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` supports `cache: true` with `cache-dependency-path`.

### WARNING - Action versions not pinned to SHA

`actions/checkout@v4` and `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` use major version tags, not SHA pins. A compromised tag could inject malicious code. For higher security, pin to full commit SHA:

```yaml
uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683  # v4.2.2
```

This applies to all three workflows.

### 1. App-of-Apps Pattern

**[WARNING] Root app has no retry strategy.**
File: `infra/k8s/argocd/root-app.yaml`, lines 17-20.
The root application lacks `retry` and `syncOptions` that all child apps have. If the root app fails to sync (e.g., transient Git fetch error), there is no automatic retry. Since this is the single point of entry for the entire platform, it should be the most resilient application.

```yaml
  backoff:

    factor: 2
```

Since the root app manages Application CRDs in the `argocd` namespace, server-side apply avoids ownership conflicts if ArgoCD itself or another controller also modifies these resources.

### 2. Application Sync Policies

Every application in `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/` consistently specifies:

```yaml
    selfHeal: true

```
This is correct for a dev/local environment -- drift is auto-corrected and orphaned resources are cleaned up.

**[GOOD] All child applications have consistent retry strategies.**
Every child app uses `limit: 5` with exponential backoff (`5s`, factor `2`, max `3m`). This is well-tuned for transient failures.

None of the applications specify `RespectIgnoreDifferences=true` in their `syncOptions`. 3. ApiService depends on PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, and Keycloak

# Comprehensive Dockerfile and CI/CD Workflow Review

## Files Reviewed

- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/.github/workflows/release.yml`

## DOCKERFILE: `src/MicroCommerce.Web/Dockerfile`

### GOOD - Multi-stage build with proper separation

The Dockerfile uses a clean 3-stage build pattern (deps, builder, runner). Dependencies are copied before source code, which maximizes layer caching. The `npm ci --ignore-scripts` in the deps stage ensures reproducible installs and avoids malicious post-install scripts.

### GOOD - Non-root user in production stage

Lines 33-41 correctly create a dedicated `nextjs` user (UID 1001) and switch to it before running the application. Files are copied with `--chown=nextjs:nodejs`. This is solid.

### GOOD - Next.js standalone output mode

The `next.config.ts` has `output: 'standalone'` configured, and the Dockerfile correctly copies `standalone` and `static` directories. This produces a minimal production image.

### GOOD - Telemetry disabled

`NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` is set in both the build and runner stages, preventing data leakage.

### WARNING - Hardcoded placeholder secret in build layer (lines 18-19)

```dockerfile
ENV AUTH_SECRET=placeholder-for-docker-build
ENV AUTH_TRUST_HOST=true
```

While the comment explains this is a placeholder overridden at runtime, `ENV` directives are baked into the image layer metadata. Anyone who pulls the image can inspect it with `docker inspect` and see these values. Although the value is "placeholder", this sets a bad precedent and could confuse operators into thinking this is the actual secret. Use `ARG` instead of `ENV` for build-time-only values, since `ARG` values are not persisted in the final image layers:

```dockerfile
ARG AUTH_SECRET=placeholder-for-docker-build
ARG AUTH_TRUST_HOST=true
```

### WARNING - No HEALTHCHECK instruction

The Dockerfile has no `HEALTHCHECK` directive. While Kubernetes liveness/readiness probes typically handle this, a Dockerfile-level health check is valuable for standalone Docker usage and `docker compose` environments. Consider adding:

```dockerfile
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=5s \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1
```

### INFO - Consider pinning the Node.js image digest

`node:22-alpine` is a floating tag. For fully reproducible builds, consider pinning to a digest:

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine@sha256:<digest> AS base
```

This prevents silent base image changes between builds. Dependabot can update the digest automatically.

### INFO - No .npm cache mount for faster CI rebuilds

The `npm ci` step could benefit from a BuildKit cache mount to speed up rebuilds:

```dockerfile
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.npm npm ci --ignore-scripts
```

---

## .NET Container Publishing (via `dotnet publish /t:PublishContainer`)

### GOOD - Chiseled base image

`Directory.Build.props` line 13 specifies `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:10.0-noble-chiseled-extra` as the base image. Chiseled images are rootless, distroless, and have a minimal attack surface. This is an excellent security choice.

### GOOD - Container configuration centralized in Directory.Build.props

The `ContainerRegistry` and `ContainerBaseImage` properties are defined once in `Directory.Build.props`, and per-service `ContainerRepository` values are in individual `.csproj` files. This is clean and maintainable.

### INFO - No ReadyToRun or trimming configured

The `.csproj` files and `Directory.Build.props` do not enable `PublishReadyToRun`, `PublishTrimmed`, or Native AOT. For a microservices project, enabling ReadyToRun (`<PublishReadyToRun>true</PublishReadyToRun>`) would improve cold-start performance at the cost of slightly larger images. Full trimming or AOT may not be feasible given the heavy use of reflection-based libraries (MediatR, EF Core, MassTransit), so this is informational only.

---

## WORKFLOW: `.github/workflows/dotnet-test.yml`

### CRITICAL - .NET SDK version mismatch

Line 30 specifies `dotnet-version: 9.0.x`, but the project targets `net10.0` (confirmed in `Directory.Build.props` line 4 and all `.csproj` files). This means the test workflow will fail on every run because .NET 9 cannot build .NET 10 projects. This must be updated to `10.0.x`.

### CRITICAL - Aspire workload install is fragile and slow

Lines 33-35:
```yaml
- name: Setup Aspire
  run: |
    dotnet workload update
    dotnet workload install aspire
```

`dotnet workload update` updates ALL workloads, which is slow and non-deterministic. The Aspire workload version is not pinned, so different runs may get different versions. Consider pinning:

```yaml
- name: Setup Aspire
  run: dotnet workload install aspire --version 9.3.0
```

Or better, use the `aspire` workload manifest pinning from a `global.json` file.

### WARNING - No permissions block

The workflow has no `permissions:` declaration. By default, `GITHUB_TOKEN` gets broad read/write permissions. Add a least-privilege permissions block:

```yaml
permissions:
  contents: read
```

### WARNING - No NuGet package caching

The workflow restores NuGet packages from scratch on every run. Add caching:

```yaml
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.nuget/packages
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-nuget-${{ hashFiles('**/*.csproj', '**/Directory.Build.props') }}
    restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-nuget-
```

Alternatively, `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` supports `cache: true` with `cache-dependency-path`.

## ArgoCD GitOps Configuration Review

### Scope

All files under `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/argocd/` plus the bootstrap script and related Kustomize manifests.

**Files reviewed:**

- `infra/k8s/argocd/root-app.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/apiservice.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/gateway.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/web.yaml`

- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/rabbitmq.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/postgres.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/otel-collector.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/aspire-dashboard.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/argocd-cmd-params-cm.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/argocd/argocd-server-nodeport.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/bootstrap.sh`

---

### 1. App-of-Apps Pattern

**[GOOD] Properly implemented app-of-apps pattern.**
The `root-app.yaml` points to `infra/k8s/argocd/apps` which contains individual Application CRDs. This is the correct structure -- a single root application manages child applications declaratively via Git.

**[WARNING] Root app has no retry strategy.**
File: `infra/k8s/argocd/root-app.yaml`, lines 17-20.
The root application lacks `retry` and `syncOptions` that all child apps have. If the root app fails to sync (e.g., transient Git fetch error), there is no automatic retry. Since this is the single point of entry for the entire platform, it should be the most resilient application.

```yaml
# Missing from root-app.yaml:
syncOptions:
- CreateNamespace=true
retry:
  limit: 5
  backoff:
    duration: 5s
    factor: 2
    maxDuration: 3m
```

**[INFO] Consider adding `ServerSideApply=true` to root app syncOptions.**
Since the root app manages Application CRDs in the `argocd` namespace, server-side apply avoids ownership conflicts if ArgoCD itself or another controller also modifies these resources.

---

### 2. Application Sync Policies

**[GOOD] All child applications have automated sync with selfHeal and prune.**
Every application in `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/` consistently specifies:
```yaml
syncPolicy:
  automated:
    selfHeal: true
    prune: true
```
This is correct for a dev/local environment -- drift is auto-corrected and orphaned resources are cleaned up.

**[GOOD] All child applications have consistent retry strategies.**
Every child app uses `limit: 5` with exponential backoff (`5s`, factor `2`, max `3m`). This is well-tuned for transient failures.

**[WARNING] No `Replace=true` or `ServerSideApply=true` sync option for stateful resources.**
File: `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/postgres.yaml`, line 21.
PostgreSQL uses a StatefulSet with `volumeClaimTemplates`. Certain fields on StatefulSets are immutable after creation. Without `ServerSideApply=true` or careful `ignoreDifferences`, ArgoCD may report the application as OutOfSync permanently if Kubernetes adds default values to the StatefulSet spec. This becomes a real problem when you modify resource requests/limits or add containers.

**[WARNING] Missing `RespectIgnoreDifferences=true` sync option.**
None of the applications specify `RespectIgnoreDifferences=true` in their `syncOptions`. Without this, even if you later add `ignoreDifferences`, ArgoCD will still attempt to "fix" those differences during automated syncs. This option is needed for the `ignoreDifferences` feature to work properly with automated sync.

---

### 3. Sync Waves / Hooks

**[CRITICAL] No sync wave ordering for infrastructure dependencies.**
None of the Application resources in `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/` have `argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave` annotations. The current deployment has a strict dependency chain:

1. PostgreSQL and RabbitMQ must be running first (infrastructure)
2. Keycloak must be running next (depends on its own startup, but is an auth dependency)
3. ApiService depends on PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, and Keycloak
4. Gateway depends on ApiService
5. Web depends on Gateway

Without sync waves, ArgoCD will attempt to deploy all 8 applications simultaneously. The bootstrap script works around this with sequential `kubectl wait` commands (lines 144-166), but that only covers the initial bootstrap. On subsequent syncs -- for example, after a full namespace deletion or a disaster recovery scenario -- ArgoCD has no ordering guarantees.

Recommended sync wave annotations for the Application resources:

```yaml
# postgres.yaml, rabbitmq.yaml
annotations:
  argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave: "0"

# keycloak.yaml
annotations:
  argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave: "1"

# apiservice.yaml, otel-collector.yaml, aspire-dashboard.yaml
annotations:
  argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave: "2"

# gateway.yaml
annotations:
  argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave: "3"

# web.yaml
annotations:
  argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave: "4"
```

**[INFO] No PreSync/PostSync hooks defined.**
There are no ArgoCD hooks (e.g., database migration Jobs as `PreSync` hooks, smoke test Jobs as `PostSync` hooks). For the current scope this is acceptable, but as the platform matures, consider adding a `PreSync` Job for database migrations rather than running them inside the ApiService container at startup.

---

### 4. Health Checks

**[INFO] No custom health assessments defined.**
ArgoCD relies on its built-in health checks for standard Kubernetes resources (Deployment, StatefulSet, Service). For this project, the built-in checks are sufficient since all workloads are standard resource types. No custom CRDs requiring custom health Lua scripts were found.

**[GOOD] All workloads define Kubernetes health probes.**
The underlying deployments (`apiservice`, `keycloak`, `postgres`) all define `readinessProbe`, `livenessProbe`, and in some cases `startupProbe`. This means ArgoCD's health assessment will correctly reflect actual application health.

---

### 5. RBAC / AppProject

**[CRITICAL] All applications use the `default` AppProject with no restrictions.**
Every Application resource specifies `project: default` (e.g., `infra/k8s/argocd/root-app.yaml` line 9, and line 9 of every child app). The `default` AppProject permits:

- **Any source repository** -- a compromised Application CRD could point to a malicious repo
- **Any destination cluster and namespace** -- applications could deploy to `kube-system`, `argocd`, or any other namespace
- **Any resource kind** -- applications could create ClusterRoles, ClusterRoleBindings, or other cluster-scoped resources

You should define a dedicated `AppProject` that restricts:

```yaml
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AppProject
metadata:
  name: micro-commerce
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  description: MicroCommerce platform
  sourceRepos:
  - https://github.com/baotoq/micro-commerce.git
  destinations:
  - namespace: micro-commerce
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
  - namespace: argocd
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
  clusterResourceWhitelist:
  - group: ''
    kind: Namespace
  namespaceResourceBlacklist:
  - group: ''
    kind: ResourceQuota
  - group: ''
    kind: LimitRange
```

Then change all Application resources to `project: micro-commerce`.

---

### 6. ignoreDifferences

**[WARNING] No `ignoreDifferences` configured on any application.**
Several resources are susceptible to perpetual OutOfSync states due to Kubernetes mutating admission controllers and defaulting:

- **StatefulSet (postgres):** Kubernetes may add default `volumeClaimTemplates.spec.volumeMode: Filesystem` and `resources` defaults, causing drift.
- **ConfigMap (keycloak-realm):** The Kustomize `configMapGenerator` appends a hash suffix to the ConfigMap name. While Kustomize handles the reference update, ArgoCD may show the old-named ConfigMap as needing pruning after regeneration.

At minimum, consider adding to the postgres Application:

```yaml
spec:
  ignoreDifferences:
  - group: apps
    kind: StatefulSet
    jsonPointers:
    - /spec/volumeClaimTemplates/0/spec/resources
```

---

### 7. Resource Tracking

**[INFO] No explicit resource tracking method configured.**
ArgoCD defaults to annotation-based tracking (`argocd.argoproj.io/tracking-id`). For this project's scale, the default is fine. For larger deployments, label-based tracking or the newer server-side tracking method can reduce annotation size and improve performance.

---

### 8. Multi-Environment Support

**[WARNING] Only `dev` overlay exists; hardcoded to `master` branch for all applications.**
Every Application targets `targetRevision: master`. This means:

- There is no staging or production environment separation
- There is no way to promote a specific Git SHA through environments
- All applications will always track the latest `master` commit

For a showcase project this is acceptable, but the structure is partially prepared for multi-environment (the `overlays/dev/` directory exists). To complete the pattern:

- Create `overlays/staging/` and `overlays/prod/` directories
- Create corresponding ArgoCD Application sets per environment, or use an `ApplicationSet` with a Git directory generator
- Use `targetRevision` to pin specific tags or SHAs for non-dev environments

**[WARNING] Inconsistent use of base vs overlay paths.**
Some applications point to overlays, others point directly to base:

| Application | Path | Issue |
|---|---|---|
| `apiservice` | `infra/k8s/overlays/dev/apiservice` | Correct -- uses overlay |
| `gateway` | `infra/k8s/overlays/dev/gateway` | Correct -- uses overlay |
| `web` | `infra/k8s/overlays/dev/web` | Correct -- uses overlay |
| `keycloak` | `infra/k8s/base/keycloak` | Points directly to base |
| `rabbitmq` | `infra/k8s/base/rabbitmq` | Points directly to base |
| `postgres` | `infra/k8s/base/postgres` | Points directly to base |
| `otel-collector` | `infra/k8s/base/otel-collector` | Points directly to base |
| `aspire-dashboard` | `infra/k8s/base/aspire-dashboard` | Points directly to base |

The infrastructure services (postgres, rabbitmq, keycloak) point directly to `base/`. If you ever need environment-specific configuration for these (different resource limits, replica counts, credentials), you will need to create overlays and update the Application paths. The inconsistency makes the mental model harder to follow.

---

### 9. Secret Management Integration

**[GOOD] Sealed Secrets properly integrated with bootstrap workflow.**
The `bootstrap.sh` script (lines 32-83) installs the Sealed Secrets controller, generates sealed secrets with `kubeseal`, and applies them before ArgoCD takes over. This is a sound approach -- secrets are sealed at bootstrap time and the raw secrets never appear in Git.

**[CRITICAL] Sealed secret YAML files are not committed to Git.**
The `seal_secret` function in `bootstrap.sh` (lines 47-63) writes sealed secret files to the local filesystem (e.g., `base/postgres/sealed-secret.yaml`) but these files are generated at bootstrap time and are not tracked in Git. Examining the kustomization files:

- `infra/k8s/base/postgres/kustomization.yaml` references only `statefulset.yaml` and `service.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/base/rabbitmq/kustomization.yaml` references only `deployment.yaml` and `service.yaml`
- `infra/k8s/base/keycloak/kustomization.yaml` references only `deployment.yaml` and `service.yaml`

The sealed secrets are `kubectl apply`-ed directly in the bootstrap script (lines 81-83) outside of ArgoCD's management. This means:

1. **Secrets are not GitOps-managed** -- ArgoCD does not know about or manage these secrets
2. **If secrets are deleted**, ArgoCD cannot restore them since they are not in the tracked Git paths
3. **Secret rotation** requires manual intervention via `kubectl` rather than a Git commit

The sealed secret files should be included in the Kustomize resources and committed to Git. That is the entire point of Sealed Secrets -- the encrypted form is safe to store in version control.

**[WARNING] Dev credentials are hardcoded in bootstrap script.**
File: `bootstrap.sh`, lines 65-78. Credentials like `postgres/postgres`, `guest/guest`, `admin/admin` are plaintext in the script. While these are clearly dev-only credentials, the script sets a pattern. For non-dev environments, a different secret provisioning mechanism (e.g., Vault, external-secrets-operator) would be needed.

---

### 10. ArgoCD Server Configuration

**[WARNING] ArgoCD server running in insecure mode.**
File: `infra/k8s/argocd/argocd-cmd-params-cm.yaml`, line 7: `server.insecure: "true"`. This disables TLS on the ArgoCD server. For a local Kind cluster this is pragmatic, but this configuration file should never be applied to a non-local environment. Consider adding a comment to the file making this explicit, and ensure this config is scoped to dev only.

**[WARNING] ArgoCD exposed via NodePort without authentication context.**
File: `infra/k8s/argocd/argocd-server-nodeport.yaml`. The ArgoCD UI is exposed on NodePort 30443 mapped to host port 38443. Combined with insecure mode, this means the ArgoCD API is accessible over plaintext HTTP on the host network. For local development this is tolerable, but the configuration should be clearly segregated from any production-adjacent setup.

---

### 11. Finalizers

**[GOOD] All applications have the cascade deletion finalizer.**
Every Application resource includes:
```yaml
finalizers:
- resources-finalizer.argocd.argoproj.io
```
This ensures that when an Application is deleted from Git, ArgoCD will also delete the managed Kubernetes resources (cascade delete). This is the correct behavior for a pruning-enabled setup.

---

### Summary by Severity

| Severity | Count | Items |
|---|---|---|
| **CRITICAL** | 3 | No sync waves for dependency ordering; all apps use unrestricted `default` project; sealed secrets not in Git/Kustomize |
| **WARNING** | 6 | Root app missing retry; no `ignoreDifferences` for StatefulSet; no `RespectIgnoreDifferences` sync option; inconsistent base vs overlay paths; insecure ArgoCD server mode; hardcoded dev credentials |
| **INFO** | 3 | No server-side apply on root app; no PreSync/PostSync hooks; default resource tracking method |
| **GOOD** | 4 | App-of-apps structure; consistent sync policies; cascade deletion finalizers; Kubernetes health probes on all workloads |

1. **Add sync wave annotations** to all Application resources in `infra/k8s/argocd/apps/` to enforce deployment ordering (CRITICAL)

# Kubernetes Manifests -- Comprehensive Code Review

**Scope:** 42 files under `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/`, including base manifests, dev overlays, ArgoCD app-of-apps, kind cluster config, and the bootstrap script.

---

## CRITICAL -- Must Fix

### 1. Hardcoded secrets in plain-text deployment manifests

**File:** `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/web/deployment.yaml`, lines 30-40

```yaml
- name: AUTH_SECRET
  value: "k8s-dev-auth-secret-change-in-production"
- name: KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET
  value: "nextjs-app-secret"
```

The `AUTH_SECRET` and `KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET` are hardcoded directly in the deployment manifest. This file is committed to a public GitHub repository. While the bootstrap script properly seals secrets for PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, and Keycloak admin credentials, the web frontend secrets were overlooked entirely. Even though the AUTH_SECRET value contains a "change in production" reminder, any value committed to Git is compromised. An attacker with these values can forge NextAuth.js session tokens and authenticate as any user.

**Fix:** Create a `web-credentials` SealedSecret with `AUTH_SECRET`, `KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET`, and `KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_ID`, and reference them via `secretKeyRef` in the deployment. Add the sealing step to `bootstrap.sh`.

### 2. Default/weak credentials used for all infrastructure secrets

**File:** `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/bootstrap.sh`, lines 65-78

```bash
seal_secret "postgres-credentials" ... --from-literal=password=postgres
seal_secret "rabbitmq-credentials" ... --from-literal=password=guest
seal_secret "keycloak-credentials" ... --from-literal=admin-password=admin
```

All three infrastructure components use their default credentials (`postgres/postgres`, `guest/guest`, `admin/admin`). Although these are sealed before being applied, the actual secret values are trivially guessable defaults. The bootstrap script also prints all credentials in plain text to stdout at lines 176-178. Even for a dev/kind environment, this sets a dangerous precedent -- if this script is adapted for any other environment, those defaults carry forward.

**Fix:** Generate random passwords at bootstrap time (e.g., `openssl rand -base64 16`) and store the generated values for the operator. At minimum, add a prominent warning comment and a `--production` flag that enforces random credentials.

### 3. No securityContext on any container

Every single deployment and statefulset across all 8 workloads is missing `securityContext`. None of the containers specify:
- `runAsNonRoot: true`
- `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`
- `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`
- `capabilities: { drop: ["ALL"] }`

This means all containers run as root by default. The PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, Keycloak, and OTEL containers all have writable root filesystems, can escalate privileges, and retain all Linux capabilities.

**Affected files:**
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/postgres/statefulset.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/rabbitmq/deployment.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/keycloak/deployment.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/apiservice/deployment.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/gateway/deployment.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/web/deployment.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/otel-collector/deployment.yaml`
- `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/aspire-dashboard/deployment.yaml`

**Fix:** Add pod-level and container-level security contexts. Example for the .NET services:

```yaml
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1654    # .NET default non-root user
    fsGroup: 1654
  containers:
  - name: apiservice
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
```

### 4. Keycloak running in dev mode in "Production" setup

**File:** `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/keycloak/deployment.yaml`, line 19

```yaml
args: ["start-dev", "--import-realm"]
```

`start-dev` disables HTTPS, uses an embedded H2 database, and enables other insecure defaults. This is in the `base/` directory which is intended to be shared across environments. If a production overlay inherits this base without overriding the args, Keycloak will run in development mode in production, with no TLS, no database persistence, and relaxed security.

**Fix:** Change the base to use `start` with production flags. Create a dev overlay patch that overrides to `start-dev` only for the dev environment.

---

## WARNING -- Should Fix

### 5. RabbitMQ deployed as Deployment with no persistent storage

**File:** `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/rabbitmq/deployment.yaml`

`x.AddConsumers(typeof(Program).Assembly)` (line 115) and the `CheckoutStateMachine` saga (lines 117-123) are registered once, independent of transport. The `cfg.ConfigureEndpoints(context)` call inside both transport branches ensures MassTransit auto-creates queues/subscriptions for all consumers. This is correct and portable.

### INFO -- `KnownQueueNames` in `DeadLetterQueueService` is static and incomplete
Line 43-46 of `DeadLetterQueueService.cs` hardcodes only `"product-created-domain-event"` but the system has many more consumers (ordering, inventory, cart, etc.). This means the DLQ admin UI will only show dead letters for that one queue.

---

## 3. OTEL Observability Setup

### GOOD -- Complete OTEL pipeline (traces, metrics, logs)
The OTEL Collector configmap at `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/otel-collector/configmap.yaml` configures all three signal types (traces, metrics, logs) with the same pipeline: `otlp receiver -> memory_limiter -> batch -> otlp/aspire exporter`. This ensures nothing is dropped silently.

### GOOD -- Memory limiter configuration
The `memory_limiter` processor (lines 16-19) is properly configured with `limit_percentage: 80` and `spike_limit_percentage: 25`. This prevents the collector from OOMing under heavy telemetry load.

### GOOD -- Health check extension exposed
The collector exposes a health check on port 13133 (line 32), and both liveness and readiness probes are configured in the deployment. This ensures Kubernetes can detect and restart an unhealthy collector.

### GOOD -- Service instrumentation is comprehensive
In `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/MicroCommerce.ServiceDefaults/Extensions.cs`:
- ASP.NET Core instrumentation for HTTP traces and metrics
- HttpClient instrumentation for outgoing calls
- Runtime instrumentation for GC/thread metrics
- MassTransit trace source registered (line 66) for message bus visibility
- Health check endpoints filtered from traces (lines 69-71) to reduce noise
- Conditional OTLP exporter (lines 86-91) only activates when `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` is set

### GOOD -- OTEL environment variables properly set in K8s deployments
Both `apiservice` and `gateway` deployments set `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` pointing to `http://otel-collector:4317` and `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME`. This correctly routes telemetry through the collector to the Aspire Dashboard.

### WARNING -- Aspire Dashboard has no authentication in K8s
The Aspire Dashboard deployment at `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/infra/k8s/base/aspire-dashboard/deployment.yaml` sets `DOTNET_DASHBOARD_UNSECURED_ALLOW_ANONYMOUS=true` and `DASHBOARD__OTLP__AUTHMODE=Unsecured` (lines 26-29). Combined with the NodePort service exposing it on port 30888 (mapped to host port 38888), anyone with network access to the kind cluster can view all traces, logs, and metrics. For a local dev cluster this is acceptable, but this configuration should never be used in shared or production environments.

### INFO -- No resource attributes configured
The OTEL configuration does not add resource attributes beyond `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME`. Adding `service.namespace`, `service.version`, and `deployment.environment` resource attributes would improve trace correlation and filtering in the Aspire Dashboard.

### INFO -- Web frontend not instrumented with OTEL
The Next.js frontend (`web` deployment) does not have `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` set, so server-side rendering traces and frontend performance metrics are not captured. Next.js supports OpenTelemetry via the `@vercel/otel` or `next-otel` packages.

### INFO -- Batch processor settings are reasonable
The batch processor with `timeout: 5s` and `send_batch_size: 1024` provides a good balance between latency and efficiency for a dev environment.

---

## 4. Runtime API URL Resolution

### GOOD -- Dual-mode resolution pattern
The `getApiBaseUrl()` function in `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/MicroCommerce.Web/src/lib/config.ts` correctly reads Aspire-injected environment variables (`services__gateway__https__0` or `services__gateway__http__0`) first, then falls back to `API_URL`. This allows the same code to work in both Aspire and standalone dev modes.

### GOOD -- `/api/config` route exposes server-side URL to client
The route handler at `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/MicroCommerce.Web/src/app/api/config/route.ts` wraps `getApiBaseUrl()` in a JSON response, allowing client-side code to discover the API URL dynamically without baking it into the JavaScript bundle.

### GOOD -- Singleton promise pattern for client-side resolution
In `/Users/baotoq/Work/micro-commerce/src/MicroCommerce.Web/src/lib/api.ts`, the `getApiBase()` function (lines 4-17) caches the config fetch in `_configPromise`, ensuring only one HTTP request to `/api/config` regardless of how many API calls are made. This is efficient and avoids a waterfall of config requests.

### CRITICAL -- Client-side API calls bypass the Gateway in K8s
In the K8s deployment, the web frontend's server-side `getApiBaseUrl()` resolves to `http://gateway:8080` (from `services__gateway__https__0` env var in the web deployment). However, the `/api/config` route **exposes this internal cluster URL to the browser**. When client-side JavaScript runs `fetch("http://gateway:8080/api/catalog/products")`, this will fail because `gateway:8080` is a Kubernetes internal DNS name that is not resolvable from the user's browser.

Looking at line 41-42 of the web K8s deployment:
```yaml
- name: services__gateway__https__0
  value: "http://gateway:8080"
```

The `getApiBaseUrl()` returns `"http://gateway:8080"`, and `/api/config` sends this to the browser. The browser cannot reach `gateway:8080`.

**Impact:** All client-side API calls (every `getApiBase()` call in `api.ts` that runs in the browser) will fail in the K8s deployment. This breaks the entire storefront for any interactive features.

**Recommendation:** The `/api/config` route should return a browser-reachable URL (e.g., `http://localhost:38800`) rather than the internal service URL. Either:
1. Add a separate env var like `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` set to the external gateway URL and return that from `/api/config`, OR
2. Return a relative URL (empty string) so client fetches go through the Next.js server which can proxy to the gateway, OR
3. Make `getApiBaseUrl()` aware of the distinction between server-side and client-facing URLs.

### WARNING -- SSR-side `fetch` in `api.ts` uses the same `getApiBase()` as client-side
The server-side rendering code path (line 12-13 of `api.ts`) falls back to `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL || "http://localhost:5200"`. In K8s, `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` is not set in the web deployment manifest, so SSR calls from the Next.js server that happen to take this path would hit localhost:5200 (which does not exist in the container). However, for server components that `import { getApiBaseUrl } from "@/lib/config"` directly (using the config.ts function), they would correctly get `http://gateway:8080`. The dual-path approach creates confusion about which resolution function to use server-side.

### WARNING -- No cache-busting or TTL on the config promise
Once `_configPromise` is set (line 6 of `api.ts`), it is never reset. If the API URL changes (for example during a rolling deployment or DNS failover), the client will keep using the stale URL until the page is fully refreshed. For a long-lived SPA session, this could cause silent failures.

### INFO -- The `services__gateway__https__0` env var name contains "https" but points to HTTP
In the web K8s deployment (line 41), the env var is named `services__gateway__https__0` but its value is `"http://gateway:8080"`. While this is just a naming convention from Aspire's service discovery and the code reads both `https__0` and `http__0`, it could confuse operators maintaining the K8s manifests.

---

## Summary Table

| # | Severity | Area | Finding |
|---|----------|------|---------|
| 1 | **CRITICAL** | API URL Resolution | `/api/config` exposes internal K8s service URL (`gateway:8080`) to browser; all client-side API calls fail in K8s |
| 2 | **WARNING** | Bootstrap | No dependency checks for required CLI tools (kind, kubectl, kubeseal, dotnet, docker) |
| 3 | **WARNING** | Bootstrap | No trap/cleanup handler on script failure; partial state left behind |
| 4 | **WARNING** | Bootstrap | No kubectl context guard; weak dev credentials could be applied to wrong cluster |
| 5 | **WARNING** | Bootstrap | `sleep 10` for ArgoCD sync is fragile; `kubectl wait` may fail if pods not yet created |
| 6 | **WARNING** | MassTransit | EF Core outbox only on CatalogDbContext; domain events from other features lack at-least-once delivery guarantee |
| 7 | **WARNING** | MassTransit | RabbitMQ credentials use Kubernetes env var expansion which is ordering-dependent |
| 8 | **WARNING** | OTEL | Aspire Dashboard exposed unauthenticated via NodePort; acceptable for local dev only |
| 9 | **WARNING** | API URL | SSR vs client-side URL resolution uses two different functions with different fallback behavior |
| 10 | **WARNING** | API URL | Config promise has no TTL; stale URL cached indefinitely in browser |
| 11 | **INFO** | Bootstrap | Image builds are sequential; could be parallelized |
| 12 | **INFO** | MassTransit | `KnownQueueNames` only lists one queue; DLQ UI will miss dead letters on other queues |
| 13 | **INFO** | OTEL | No OTEL resource attributes beyond service name |
| 14 | **INFO** | OTEL | Next.js frontend not instrumented with OpenTelemetry |
| 15 | **INFO** | API URL | Env var named `services__gateway__https__0` but value is HTTP |
| 16 | **GOOD** | Bootstrap | `set -euo pipefail`, idempotent operations, color-coded logging |
| 17 | **GOOD** | Bootstrap | Comprehensive probe configuration on all K8s deployments |
| 18 | **GOOD** | MassTransit | Clean transport switching with env var + conditional branching |
| 19 | **GOOD** | MassTransit | NoOp DLQ fallback for RabbitMQ environments |
| 20 | **GOOD** | MassTransit | ASB-specific DLQ config guarded by type check |
| 21 | **GOOD** | OTEL | Full trace/metric/log pipeline with memory limiter and batching |
| 22 | **GOOD** | OTEL | MassTransit trace source registered for message bus observability |
| 23 | **GOOD** | OTEL | Health endpoints filtered from traces |
| 24 | **GOOD** | API URL | Singleton promise avoids redundant config fetches |

The single **critical** issue (finding #1) will cause the frontend to be non-functional in Kubernetes for any client-side rendered page making API calls. This should be addressed before any K8s deployment is considered usable.</result>

# v3.0 Kubernetes & GitOps — Deep Implementation Audit

## CRITICAL Findings (11)

| # | Area | Finding |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **K8s Manifests** | Web frontend secrets (`AUTH_SECRET`, `KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET`) hardcoded in plaintext in `base/web/deployment.yaml` — committed to public repo |
| 2 | **K8s Manifests** | No `securityContext` on any of 8 workloads — all containers run as root with full capabilities |
| 3 | **K8s Manifests** | Keycloak uses `start-dev` in `base/` — inheritable by production overlays |
| 4 | **K8s Manifests** | Default/weak credentials (`postgres/postgres`, `guest/guest`, `admin/admin`) for all infrastructure |
| 5 | **ArgoCD** | No sync wave annotations — all 8 apps deploy simultaneously with no dependency ordering |
| 6 | **ArgoCD** | All apps use unrestricted `default` AppProject — no source/destination restrictions |
| 7 | **ArgoCD** | Sealed secrets not committed to Git or managed by Kustomize — ArgoCD can't restore them |
| 8 | **CI/CD** | `.NET SDK 9.0.x` in `dotnet-test.yml` and `release.yml` but project targets `net10.0` — tests won't run |
| 9 | **CI/CD** | `release.yml` references stale project paths (`CartService`, `Gateway/Yarp`) that don't exist |
| 10 | **CI/CD** | Fragile Aspire workload install — no version pinning |
| 11 | **API URL** | `/api/config` exposes internal K8s URL (`gateway:8080`) to browser — all client-side API calls fail in K8s |

## WARNING Findings (23)

| # | Area | Finding |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **K8s** | RabbitMQ deployed as Deployment with no persistent storage — message loss on restart |
| 2 | **K8s** | Missing startup probes on gateway, web, rabbitmq, otel-collector, aspire-dashboard |
| 3 | **K8s** | No service accounts — all pods use `default` SA |
| 4 | **K8s** | Dev overlay missing otel-collector and aspire-dashboard |
| 5 | **K8s** | No `imagePullPolicy` specified — implicit behavior varies by tag |
| 6 | **K8s** | No PodDisruptionBudgets |
| 7 | **K8s** | No ResourceQuota/LimitRange on namespace |
| 8 | **K8s** | Hardcoded `namespace: micro-commerce` in base manifests defeats Kustomize transformer |
| 9 | **ArgoCD** | Root app missing retry strategy |
| 10 | **ArgoCD** | No `ignoreDifferences` for PostgreSQL StatefulSet |
| 11 | **ArgoCD** | No `RespectIgnoreDifferences` sync option |
| 12 | **ArgoCD** | Inconsistent base vs overlay paths (infra→base, apps→overlay) |
| 13 | **ArgoCD** | ArgoCD server running insecure mode in base config |
| 14 | **ArgoCD** | Hardcoded dev credentials in bootstrap script |
| 15 | **CI/CD** | No `permissions:` block in test and release workflows |
| 16 | **CI/CD** | No NuGet caching in any workflow |
| 17 | **CI/CD** | No path filtering on `container-images.yml` — rebuilds on doc changes |
| 18 | **CI/CD** | Tests commented out in `release.yml` |
| 19 | **CI/CD** | Dockerfile uses `ENV` for build-time placeholder secret (should be `ARG`) |
| 20 | **Bootstrap** | No dependency checks for required CLI tools |
| 21 | **Bootstrap** | No trap/cleanup on failure |
| 22 | **Bootstrap** | `sleep 10` for ArgoCD sync is fragile |
| 23 | **MassTransit** | EF Core outbox only on `CatalogDbContext` — other features lack at-least-once delivery |

## GOOD Findings (20)

| Area | What's Working Well |
|------|-------------------|
| **K8s** | Proper Kustomize base/overlay structure, health probes on all workloads, resource requests/limits everywhere, PostgreSQL as StatefulSet with PVC, SealedSecrets integration |
| **ArgoCD** | App-of-apps pattern, consistent automated sync + selfHeal + prune, cascade deletion finalizers, retry with exponential backoff |
| **CI/CD** | Multi-stage Dockerfile, non-root user in web image, chiseled .NET base images, GITHUB_TOKEN auth, Docker layer caching, multi-arch images, Next.js standalone output |
| **Bootstrap** | `set -euo pipefail`, idempotent operations, color-coded logging, comprehensive final summary |
| **MassTransit** | Clean transport switching, NoOp DLQ fallback, ASB-specific config guarded by type check |
| **OTEL** | Full trace/metric/log pipeline, memory limiter, MassTransit traces, health endpoint filtering |
| **API URL** | Singleton promise pattern avoids redundant config fetches |

## Priority Remediation Order

**Tier 1 — Broken right now:**
1. Fix .NET SDK version in CI workflows (`9.0.x` → `10.0.x`)
2. Fix stale project paths in `release.yml`
3. Fix client-side API URL resolution (browser gets unreachable `gateway:8080`)

**Tier 2 — Security exposure:**
4. Move web frontend secrets to SealedSecret
5. Add `securityContext` to all workloads
6. Move Keycloak `start-dev` to dev overlay only
7. Create dedicated ArgoCD `AppProject` with restricted permissions
8. Commit sealed secrets to Git for ArgoCD management

**Tier 3 — Reliability:**
9. Add sync wave annotations for dependency ordering
10. Add persistent storage for RabbitMQ
11. Add startup probes to remaining workloads
12. Fix MassTransit outbox to cover all DbContexts
13. Add bootstrap script dependency checks and error traps

---

Want me to turn these findings into a new milestone with requirements and a roadmap? That would bring us back to the `/gsd:new-milestone` flow.
Since these are fixes and improvements to v3.0 K8s & GitOps, **v3.1** makes sense. Let me initialize the milestone.

# MicroCommerce

## What This Is

A showcase e-commerce platform demonstrating modern .NET microservices architecture with DDD tactical patterns. Users can browse products, manage profiles, write verified reviews, save wishlists, add items to cart, and complete purchases through a polished Next.js storefront backed by a modular monolith with event-driven architecture, CQRS, and a comprehensive DDD building block foundation (strongly-typed IDs, SmartEnums, Result pattern, Specifications, interceptor-driven audit/concurrency/soft-delete).

## Core Value

**A user can complete a purchase end-to-end** — browse products, add to cart, checkout as a guest, and see their order confirmation. Everything else supports this flow.

## Requirements

### Validated

- ✓ Product catalog with categories, search, and infinite scroll — v1.0
- ✓ Product detail pages with stock display and add-to-cart — v1.0
- ✓ Shopping cart (persistent, guest-friendly, optimistic UI) — v1.0
- ✓ Checkout flow with mock payment and saga orchestration — v1.0
- ✓ Order confirmation and order history with real-time status — v1.0
- ✓ Inventory tracking with stock reservations and TTL — v1.0
- ✓ Admin UI for product management, inventory, and orders — v1.0
- ✓ Admin dashboard with stat cards, charts, and Kanban board — v1.0
- ✓ Seed data with 50 products across 8 categories — v1.0
- ✓ API Gateway (YARP) with JWT auth and rate limiting — v1.0
- ✓ Event-driven communication (MassTransit + Azure Service Bus) — v1.0
- ✓ CQRS pattern with MediatR and validation pipeline — v1.0
- ✓ Database-per-feature isolation with separate DbContexts — v1.0
- ✓ 180 automated tests (unit, integration, E2E) — v1.0
- ✓ Keycloak authentication (backend JWT + NextAuth.js) — existing
- ✓ .NET Aspire orchestration — existing
- ✓ DDD building blocks (aggregates, domain events, value objects) — existing
- ✓ OpenTelemetry observability — existing
- ✓ User profiles with display name, avatar, address book — v1.1
- ✓ Guest-to-authenticated cart and order merge on login — v1.1
- ✓ Product reviews with verified purchase enforcement (star ratings + text) — v1.1
- ✓ Aggregate star ratings and review counts on product pages — v1.1
- ✓ Wishlists with add/remove, move-to-cart, heart icon indicators — v1.1
- ✓ E2E test coverage for user features (guest, authenticated, navigation) — v1.1
- ✓ DDD audit (71 findings) with value object migration to record structs — v1.1
- ✓ CQRS compliance fixes and aggregate boundary enforcement — v1.1
- ✓ Entity hierarchy (Entity<TId>, AuditableAggregateRoot<TId>) with typed ID child entities — v2.0
- ✓ Vogen source-generated strongly-typed IDs (14 types) with auto EF Core + JSON converters — v2.0
- ✓ EF Core conventions for StronglyTypedId auto-conversion and ConcurrencyToken auto-config — v2.0
- ✓ SmartEnum state machines for OrderStatus (8 states) and ProductStatus (3 states) — v2.0
- ✓ Result pattern (FluentResults) with dual MediatR validation pipeline and HTTP mapping — v2.0
- ✓ Specification pattern (Ardalis.Specification) with 8 composable query specs — v2.0
- ✓ AuditInterceptor auto-setting CreatedAt/UpdatedAt on IAuditable entities — v2.0
- ✓ ConcurrencyInterceptor with explicit Version column replacing xmin — v2.0
- ✓ SoftDeleteInterceptor infrastructure wired (ready for future entity adoption) — v2.0
- ✓ Integration testing with Testcontainers + WebApplicationFactory (182 tests) — v2.0
- ✓ Full building block adoption across all 7 feature modules — v2.0
- ✓ OpenAPI schema transformers for Vogen IDs and SmartEnums — v2.0
- ✓ Obsolete ValueObject base class and StronglyTypedIdConvention removed — v2.0
- ✓ Dockerfiles for ApiService (chiseled .NET), Gateway (chiseled .NET), Web (standalone Node.js) — v3.0
- ✓ Kustomize manifests (base + dev overlay) for all services and infrastructure — v3.0
- ✓ Full stack deployment in K8s: apps + PostgreSQL + Keycloak + RabbitMQ — v3.0
- ✓ RabbitMQ transport support for MassTransit (MASSTRANSIT_TRANSPORT env var switching) — v3.0
- ✓ GitHub Actions CI pipeline building and pushing images to ghcr.io — v3.0
- ✓ ArgoCD with app-of-apps pattern for GitOps deployment (self-heal + prune) — v3.0
- ✓ Sealed Secrets for K8s secret management — v3.0
- ✓ CI/CD GitOps loop: CI commits SHA tags → ArgoCD auto-syncs — v3.0
- ✓ OTEL Collector + Aspire Dashboard deployed in K8s for monitoring — v3.0
- ✓ Local kind cluster as dev environment with bootstrap.sh — v3.0
- ✓ Complete UI refresh: 11 pages restyled with shadcn/ui, oklch tokens, DM Sans — v3.0
- ✓ Runtime API URL resolution for client-side K8s deployment — v3.0

### Active

(No active milestone — use `/gsd:new-milestone` to start next)

### Out of Scope

- Real payment processing (Stripe, etc.) — mock payments sufficient for demo
- Event sourcing — adds complexity without proportional demo value
- Separate admin application — integrated admin routes simpler
- Mobile app — web-first, responsive design covers mobile
- Multi-tenancy — single store demonstration
- Internationalization — English only for now
- Anonymous reviews — trust collapse risk; verified purchase enforcement is core
- Public user profiles — privacy concern; profiles are private to account owner
- AI review summaries — requires LLM integration, cost analysis
- Review image moderation — requires moderation service
- Generic Repository wrapper — EF Core DbContext already is unit-of-work + repository
- Event sourcing primitives — adds complexity without proportional demo value
- CreatedBy/ModifiedBy user tracking — requires IHttpContextAccessor integration, defer to future

## Context

**Shipped v3.0 Kubernetes & GitOps** with 25.6K LOC C# backend, 15.7K LOC TypeScript frontend, 1.3K LOC K8s manifests. Full stack deployed to kind cluster via ArgoCD GitOps.

**Tech stack:**
- Backend: .NET 10, ASP.NET Core Minimal APIs, .NET Aspire 13.1.0
- Frontend: Next.js 16, React 19, TypeScript 5, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS v4
- Auth: Keycloak (backend JWT + NextAuth.js v5 frontend)
- Database: PostgreSQL (per-feature isolation, 8 DbContexts)
- Messaging: MassTransit 9.0 (Azure Service Bus for Aspire dev, RabbitMQ for K8s)
- Gateway: YARP reverse proxy with JWT, rate limiting, CORS
- Deployment: Kubernetes (kind), Kustomize, ArgoCD, Sealed Secrets, GitHub Actions CI
- Observability: OpenTelemetry → OTEL Collector → Aspire Dashboard
- Patterns: CQRS (MediatR), DDD, Event-Driven, Vertical Slice Architecture
- DDD Building Blocks: Vogen StronglyTypedId, SmartEnum, FluentResults, Ardalis.Specification, EF Core Interceptors
- Testing: xUnit, FluentAssertions, Testcontainers, WebApplicationFactory, Playwright

**Architecture:** Modular monolith with clear bounded contexts (Catalog, Cart, Ordering, Inventory, Profiles, Reviews, Wishlists, Messaging). Full DDD building block foundation adopted across all modules. Deployed to Kubernetes via GitOps with full CI/CD automation.

**Known tech debt:**
- Hardcoded configuration (stock thresholds, shipping rates)
- Placeholder images (placehold.co URLs; Azurite images won't resolve in K8s — needs STOR-01)
- Cross-context DbContext injection in Reviews and Wishlists modules (bounded context isolation violation)
- ProductStatus missing `[JsonConverter]` attribute (server-side works, latent client-side gap)
- DomainEventInterceptor uses scoped DI (not wired via AddInterceptors — needs IPublishEndpoint)
- ISoftDeletable has zero entity adopters (infrastructure ready, no entities implement it yet)
- AUTH_URL=localhost:38800 works for local kind dev only (needs adjustment for remote clusters)
- Hero image placeholder (gray bg-muted div)

## Constraints

- **Tech Stack**: .NET 10 + Aspire for backend, Next.js 16 for frontend — committed
- **Identity**: Keycloak — integrated, don't duplicate auth
- **Message Broker**: MassTransit with Azure SB (Aspire) / RabbitMQ (K8s)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL — in deployment manifests
- **Deployment**: Kubernetes with ArgoCD GitOps

## Key Decisions

| Decision | Rationale | Outcome |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Gradual service extraction | Real-world pattern, demonstrates monolith-to-microservices journey | ✓ Good — clean module boundaries established |
| Database-per-feature | True microservices isolation, showcases data ownership | ✓ Good — 8 separate DbContexts working well |
| Guest checkout | Lower friction, simpler v1 scope | ✓ Good — cookie-based buyer identity works seamlessly |
| Mock payments | Avoid payment processor complexity while demonstrating flow | ✓ Good — toggleable success/failure |
| Cart in Postgres (not Redis) | Durability over speed, consistent with database-per-service | ✓ Good — reliable with TTL cleanup |
| Admin in main app | Simpler deployment, shared auth context | ✓ Good — route-based separation clean |
| shadcn/ui | Modern, Tailwind-based, great DX | ✓ Good — consistent design across storefront and admin |
| MassTransit + transactional outbox | Reliable event delivery with at-least-once guarantees | ✓ Good — checkout saga compensation works |
| Checkout saga (MassTransit state machine) | Orchestrate multi-step checkout with compensation | ✓ Good — handles stock reservation, payment, cart clearing |
| YARP API Gateway | Unified entry point, security centralization | ✓ Good — JWT + rate limiting at edge |
| Testcontainers for integration tests | Real database testing without Docker Compose | ✓ Good — catches real SQL/EF issues |
| ImageSharp for avatar processing | Crop-to-square and resize to 400x400 | ✓ Good — no external service needed |
| Auto-create profile on first GET | Profile always exists for authenticated users | ✓ Good — eliminates null profile edge cases |
| Silent cart merge on login | Guest cart transfers to authenticated user without intervention | ✓ Good — seamless UX |
| Verified purchase reviews | Reviews require order with Paid/Confirmed/Shipped/Delivered status | ✓ Good — trust through enforcement |
| Denormalized review stats | AverageRating and ReviewCount on Product entity | ✓ Good — fast queries, synchronous recalculation |
| Optimistic wishlist UI | Heart icon toggles immediately with cache rollback on error | ✓ Good — instant feedback |
| Readonly record structs for value objects | Compiler-generated equality, 20x faster than reflection-based ValueObject | ✓ Good — modern C# semantics |
| ComplexProperty for struct value objects | Avoids EF Core shadow primary key generation from OwnsOne | ✓ Good — cleaner mapping |
| Vernon's aggregate rules as audit standard | 4 rules for aggregate design validation | ✓ Good — caught 21 critical violations |
| Reference by identity only (Vernon Rule 3) | Product references Category by CategoryId, not navigation property | ✓ Good — proper aggregate isolation |
| Vogen for StronglyTypedId | Source-generated record structs vs hand-rolled base class | ✓ Good — zero runtime overhead, auto converters |
| SmartEnum for status types | Behavior-rich enumerations vs plain enum + switch | ✓ Good — state transitions encapsulated, no magic strings |
| FluentResults for explicit errors | Result<T> vs exceptions for domain errors | ✓ Good — 4 handlers adopted, HTTP mapping clean |
| Ardalis.Specification for queries | Composable specs vs inline LINQ | ✓ Good — 8 specs, chained composition works |
| Stateless interceptors as singletons | new() instances vs DI-resolved scoped | ✓ Good — no constructor deps, simpler wiring |
| Interceptor order: SoftDelete → Concurrency → Audit | SoftDelete before Audit so UpdatedAt set on soft-deleted entities | ✓ Good — correct behavioral ordering |
| Testcontainers over in-memory DB | Real PostgreSQL vs EF InMemory provider | ✓ Good — catches real SQL/EF issues, 38 integration tests |
| Order skips AuditableAggregateRoot | Domain-specific CreatedAt/PaidAt semantics | ✓ Good — intentional, documented design choice |

| .NET chiseled containers | Smallest secure images for ApiService/Gateway | ✓ Good — non-root by default, noble-chiseled-extra for ICU |
| Kustomize over Helm | Simpler for single-project, built into kubectl | ✓ Good — base/overlay structure clean |
| ArgoCD app-of-apps | Single root Application manages all services | ✓ Good — self-heal + prune on all 8 child apps |
| Sealed Secrets | Encrypt credentials for Git storage | ✓ Good — bootstrap generates sealed-secret.yaml at runtime |
| MassTransit transport switching | MASSTRANSIT_TRANSPORT env var for Azure SB vs RabbitMQ | ✓ Good — same consumers/saga work on both |
| NodePort for kind access | Avoid Ingress NGINX (EOL March 2026) | ✓ Good — simple port mapping chain |
| Runtime API URL resolution | getApiBase() singleton fetching /api/config at runtime | ✓ Good — single Docker image for all environments |
| OTEL Collector + Aspire Dashboard | Standalone observability without .NET Aspire AppHost | ✓ Good — distributed tracing visible in K8s |

---
*Last updated: 2026-03-03 after v3.0 milestone completed*

- **Aspire + K8s parallel paths**: Aspire stays as local inner-loop dev tool; Kustomize + ArgoCD define cluster state. No conflict.
- **RabbitMQ as K8s transport**: Azure SB emulator cannot run in K8s; `MASSTRANSIT_TRANSPORT` env var switches transport. Same consumer/saga/outbox config.
- **Kustomize over Helm**: Simpler for single-project; no templating language; built into kubectl.
- **ArgoCD v3.3.2 specifically**: v3.3.0/3.3.1 have a client-side apply migration bug.
- **Sealed Secrets from day one**: No plaintext credentials in Git ever. Key backup in cluster bootstrap script.
